label: ai
topic: Arguing a university should integrate AI education into the undergraduate curriculum
text:
When our university announced last semester that all students would receive institutional access to an AI assistant built for academic use, the reaction was revealing. Some faculty treated it as a capitulation, a tacit acknowledgement that the battle against AI in the classroom had been lost. Some students treated it as permission to outsource their problem sets entirely. Neither response was appropriate, and both demonstrated the same underlying problem: we have given students access to one of the most consequential technologies of their generation without teaching them how to think about it.

The university needs to fix this, and the fix is curricular, not procedural. Adding another paragraph to the honour code about acceptable AI use is not education. Requiring students to disclose when they have consulted an AI tool is not education. These are compliance measures, and they treat AI as a problem to be managed rather than a capability to be understood.

What would actual education look like? Two approaches, neither mutually exclusive, seem promising.

The first is a dedicated introductory course, offered to all first-year students on a pass-fail basis, structured like the writing seminars that many universities already require. Small sections, heavy on discussion, focused not on the technical mechanics of large language models but on the practical and ethical dimensions of working with AI systems. Students in a biology section would examine how AI is reshaping drug discovery and diagnostic imaging. Students in a humanities section would explore how generative models affect creative authorship and intellectual property. Students in a public policy section would debate algorithmic bias in criminal sentencing and hiring. The specifics would vary, but the core questions would be shared: what can these tools do, what can they not do, and what should we want them to do.

The second approach is modular integration within existing courses. An economics professor already teaching regression analysis could dedicate a week to how machine learning models differ from traditional econometric approaches and where each is appropriate. A literature professor could assign a generative AI to produce a critical essay on the same text the students are analysing, then lead a seminar on what the AI got right, what it got wrong, and what it could not even attempt. A chemistry professor could demonstrate AI-driven molecular modelling and discuss its limitations alongside its capabilities.

Neither approach requires hiring new faculty or building new departments. Both require the university to acknowledge that AI literacy is no longer a specialised skill but a general one, comparable to statistical reasoning or academic writing: something every educated person needs regardless of their field.

The ethical dimension deserves particular emphasis. Academic integrity cases involving AI have increased sharply across higher education in the past two years, and the pattern suggests that many students are not deliberately cheating but rather genuinely uncertain about where the boundaries lie. A student who asks an AI to help brainstorm thesis topics is engaged in a fundamentally different activity from one who asks an AI to write the thesis, but our current policies often treat both with the same vague language about unauthorised assistance. A dedicated curriculum could establish shared vocabulary and shared expectations, reducing the ambiguity that breeds accidental violations.

The university's decision to provide AI access was the right one. Pretending that students would not use these tools was never realistic, and institutional access within a governed framework is preferable to students navigating the landscape alone with personal accounts and no guidance. But access without education is like providing laboratory equipment without lab safety training: technically generous, practically negligent.

We are preparing students for careers in which AI will be a daily collaborator, a daily competitor, or both. The least we can do is ensure they understand what they are working with.
